title: The problem with public everything
instruction:
Right now, anyone can set `account.balance = -9999`. No error, no warning — just broken data.

Real bank accounts don't let you set the balance directly. There is a deposit() method, a withdraw() method — but no 'set the balance to anything you want' method.

That protection is **encapsulation** — controlling what can be accessed and changed.

Run this. See how easily the account breaks.
starter:
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner, balance):
        self.owner = owner
        self.balance = balance  # fully public — anyone can set this

account = BankAccount("Alex", 1000)
print(account.balance)  # 1000
account.balance = -9999  # no error! completely broken
print(account.balance)  # -9999
successMessage: See the problem? One line and the account is destroyed. This is why we need encapsulation — to make invalid states impossible.

title: _ convention — 'please don't touch this'
instruction:
Python's first line of encapsulation: prefix an attribute with `_` to signal it's private.

Python won't enforce it — it's a convention, a social contract. Other developers see `_balance` and know: 'that's internal, I'll use the provided methods'.

Rename `balance` to `_balance` throughout the class.

codeChecks.message: Rename to _balance: self._balance = balance (and update deposit and get_balance too!)
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: _balance says: 'internal implementation detail'. The underscore is respected by every Python developer. It doesn't lock the door — it puts a sign on it.

title: @property — read access without ()
instruction:
`@property` turns a method into an attribute that you access *without calling it*:

```python
@property
def balance(self):
    return self._balance
```

Now `account.balance` calls the method behind the scenes — but looks like attribute access. Clean.

Add the `@property` for balance.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: account.balance reads like a plain attribute but runs your method. The users of this class don't see _balance — they just see balance. Clean API, protected internals.

title: @setter — controlled write access
instruction:
Now add a setter — a method that runs when someone assigns to `balance`. The setter validates before storing.

Trying `account.balance = -500` will now trigger your validation rule.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Now account.balance = -500 is impossible. The setter rejected it. Invalid state is gone — the class enforces its own rules at assignment time.

title: Read-only property
instruction:
A property without a setter is read-only. Trying to assign to it raises `AttributeError`.

Perfect for values that are set once and should never change — like an account ID.

Add a read-only `id` property.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Read-only. No setter = no assignment allowed. ACC-0042 and it can never change. Properties give you precise control: read-only, write-once, validated, or fully flexible.

title: Build a validated User
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `User` class with full validation:
- `name` — must be a non-empty string
- `email` — must contain '@'
- `age` — must be between 0 and 120

Use `@property` and `@x.setter` for each field. Raise `ValueError` for invalid values.

Test it by trying to set bad values.
successMessage: A fully validated User class. Invalid data is impossible — the setters catch it at assignment time. This is what production-grade Python code looks like. Chapter 15 done!
